name: mako

metadata.name: Mako
metadata.summary: A super-fast templating language that borrows the best ideas from the existing templating languages.

documentation:
# Mako Templates for Python Mako is a template library written in Python. It provides a familiar, non-XML syntax which compiles into Python modules for maximum performance. Mako's syntax and API borrows from the best ideas of many others, including Django templates, Cheetah, Myghty, and Genshi. Conceptually, Mako is an embedded Python (i.e. Python Server Page) language, which refines the familiar ideas of componentized layout and inheritance to produce one of the most straightforward and flexible models available, while also maintaining close ties to Python calling and scoping semantics. ## Nutshell ``` <%inherit file="base.html"/> <% rows = [[v for v in range(0,10)] for row in range(0,10)] %> % for row in rows: ${makerow(row)} % endfor
<%def name="makerow(row)"> % for name in row: ${name}\ % endfor ``` ## Philosophy Python is a great scripting language. Don't reinvent the wheel...your templates can handle it ! ## Documentation See documentation for Mako at ## License Mako is licensed under an MIT-style license (see LICENSE). Other incorporated projects may be licensed under different licenses. All licenses allow for non-commercial and commercial use.

modules.name: mako.ext.autohandler
documentation:
adds autohandler functionality to Mako templates.

requires that the TemplateLookup class is used with templates.

usage:

```
<%!
    from mako.ext.autohandler import autohandler
%>
<%inherit file="${autohandler(template, context)}"/>
```

or with custom autohandler filename:

```
<%!
    from mako.ext.autohandler import autohandler
%>
<%inherit file="${autohandler(template, context, name='somefilename')}"/>
```
ext/autohandler.py
Copyright 2006-2024 the Mako authors and contributors <see AUTHORS file>
This module is part of Mako and is released under
the MIT License: http://www.opensource.org/licenses/mit-license.php

functions.name: mako.ext.babelplugin.extract
documentation:
Extract messages from Mako templates.

```
param fileobj

the file-like object the messages should be extracted from

param keywords

a list of keywords (i.e. function names) that should be
recognized as translation functions

param comment_tags

a list of translator tags to search for and include
in the results

param options

a dictionary of additional options (optional)

return

an iterator over (lineno, funcname, message, comments) tuples

rtype

iterator
```

functions.name: mako.filters.htmlentityreplace_errors
documentation:
An encoding error handler.

This python codecs error handler replaces unencodable
characters with HTML entities, or, if no HTML entity exists for
the character, XML character references:

```
>>> 'The cost was €12.'.encode('latin1', 'htmlentityreplace')
'The cost was €12.'
```

methods.name: mako.filters.XMLEntityEscaper.escape_entities
documentation:
Replace characters with their character entity references.

Only characters corresponding to a named entity are replaced.

methods.name: mako.filters.XMLEntityEscaper.escape
documentation:
Replace characters with their character references.

Replace characters by their named entity references.
Non-ASCII characters, if they do not have a named entity reference,
are replaced by numerical character references.

The return value is guaranteed to be ASCII.

methods.name: mako.filters.XMLEntityEscaper.unescape
documentation:
Unescape character references.

All character references (both entity references and numerical
character references) are unescaped.

functions.name: mako.testing.assertions._assert_proper_exception_context
documentation:
assert that any exception we're catching does not have a __context__
without a __cause__, and that __suppress_context__ is never set.

Python 3 will report nested as exceptions as "during the handling of
error X, error Y occurred". That's not what we want to do. We want
these exceptions in a cause chain.

functions.name: mako.testing.assertions._assert_proper_cause_cls
documentation:
assert that any exception we're catching does not have a __context__
without a __cause__, and that __suppress_context__ is never set.

Python 3 will report nested as exceptions as "during the handling of
error X, error Y occurred". That's not what we want to do. We want
these exceptions in a cause chain.

functions.name: mako.exceptions.text_error_template
documentation:
Provides a template that renders a stack trace in a similar format to
the Python interpreter, substituting source template filenames, line
numbers and code for that of the originating source template, as
applicable.

functions.name: mako.exceptions.html_error_template
documentation:
Provides a template that renders a stack trace in an HTML format,
providing an excerpt of code as well as substituting source template
filenames, line numbers and code for that of the originating source
template, as applicable.

The template's default `encoding_errors` value is
`'htmlentityreplace'`. The template has two options. With the
`full` option disabled, only a section of an HTML document is
returned. With the `css` option disabled, the default stylesheet
won't be included.

classes.name: mako.exceptions.UnsupportedError
documentation: raised when a retired feature is used.

classes.name: mako.exceptions.NameConflictError
documentation: raised when a reserved word is used inappropriately

classes.name: mako.exceptions.RichTraceback

methods.name: mako.exceptions.RichTraceback._init_message
documentation: Find a unicode representation of self.error

methods.name: mako.exceptions.RichTraceback.traceback
documentation:
Return a list of 4-tuple traceback records (i.e. normal python
format) with template-corresponding lines remapped to the originating
template.

methods.name: mako.exceptions.RichTraceback.reverse_traceback
documentation: Return the same data as traceback, except in reverse order.

methods.name: mako.exceptions.RichTraceback._init
documentation:
format a traceback from sys.exc_info() into 7-item tuples,
containing the regular four traceback tuple items, plus the original
template filename, the line number adjusted relative to the template
source, and code line from that line number of the template.

Pull the current exception from the `sys` traceback and extracts
Mako-specific template information.

See the usage examples in {ref}`handling_exceptions`.

methods.name: mako.pygen.PythonPrinter._is_unindentor

methods.name: mako.pygen.PythonPrinter._indent_line

functions.name: mako.util.parse_encoding
documentation:
Deduce the encoding of a Python source file (binary mode) from magic
comment.

It does this in the same way as the [Python interpreter](http://docs.python.org/ref/encodings.html)

The `fp` argument should be a seekable file object in binary mode.

Used by the lexer unit test to compare parse trees based on strings.

Attempt to restore the required classes to the _ast module if it
appears to be missing them

classes.name: mako.util.memoized_property
documentation: A read-only @property that is only evaluated once.

classes.name: mako.util.memoized_instancemethod
documentation:
Decorate a method memoize its return value.

Best applied to no-arg methods: memoization is not sensitive to
argument values, and will always return the same value even when
called with different arguments.

classes.name: mako.util.SetLikeDict

methods.name: mako.util.SetLikeDict.union
documentation:
produce a 'union' of this dict and another (at the key level).

values in the second dict take precedence over that of the first
a dictionary that has some setlike methods on it

classes.name: mako.util.FastEncodingBuffer
documentation:
a very rudimentary buffer that is faster than StringIO,
and supports unicode data.

classes.name: mako.util.LRUCache
documentation:
A dictionary-like object that stores a limited number of items,
discarding lesser used items periodically.

this is a rewrite of LRUCache from Myghty to use a periodic timestamp-based
paradigm so that synchronization is not really needed.  the size management
is inexact.

modules.name: mako.cache
documentation: mako/cache.py
Copyright 2006-2024 the Mako authors and contributors <see AUTHORS file>
This module is part of Mako and is released under
the MIT License: http://www.opensource.org/licenses/mit-license.php

classes.name: mako.cache.Cache

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.get_or_create
documentation:
Retrieve a value from the cache, using the given creation function
to generate a new value.

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache._ctx_get_or_create
documentation:
Retrieve a value from the cache, using the given creation function
to generate a new value.

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.set
documentation:
Place a value in the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param value

the value.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.get
documentation:
Retrieve a value from the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.  The
backend is configured using these arguments upon first request.
Subsequent requests that use the same series of configuration
values will use that same backend.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.invalidate
documentation:
Invalidate a value in the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.  The
backend is configured using these arguments upon first request.
Subsequent requests that use the same series of configuration
values will use that same backend.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.invalidate_body
documentation:
Invalidate the cached content of the "body" method for this
template.

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.invalidate_def
documentation:
Invalidate the cached content of a particular `<%def>` within this
template.

methods.name: mako.cache.Cache.invalidate_closure
documentation:
Invalidate a nested `<%def>` within this template.

Caching of nested defs is a blunt tool as there is no
management of scope -- nested defs that use cache tags
need to have names unique of all other nested defs in the
template, else their content will be overwritten by
each other.

class_variables.name: mako.cache.Cache.impl
documentation:
Provide the {class}`.CacheImpl` in use by this {class}`.Cache`.

This accessor allows a {class}`.CacheImpl` with additional
methods beyond that of {class}`.Cache` to be used programmatically.

class_variables.name: mako.cache.Cache.id
documentation:
Return the 'id' that identifies this cache.

This is a value that should be globally unique to the
{class}`.Template` associated with this cache, and can
be used by a caching system to name a local container
for data specific to this template.

class_variables.name: mako.cache.Cache.starttime
documentation:
Epochal time value for when the owning {class}`.Template` was
first compiled.

A cache implementation may wish to invalidate data earlier than
this timestamp; this has the effect of the cache for a specific
{class}`.Template` starting clean any time the {class}`.Template`
is recompiled, such as when the original template file changed on
the filesystem.

class_variables.name: mako.cache.Cache.put
documentation:
A synonym for {meth}`.Cache.set`.

This is here for backwards compatibility.

Represents a data content cache made available to the module
space of a specific {class}`.Template` object.

:::{versionadded} 0.6 :class:`.Cache` by itself is mostly a container for a :class:`.CacheImpl` object, which implements a fixed API to provide caching services; specific subclasses exist to implement different caching strategies.   Mako includes a backend that works with the Beaker caching system.   Beaker itself then supports a number of backends (i.e. file, memory, memcached, etc.)
:::

The construction of a {class}`.Cache` is part of the mechanics
of a {class}`.Template`, and programmatic access to this
cache is typically via the {attr}`.Template.cache` attribute.

classes.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl

methods.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl.get_or_create
documentation:
Retrieve a value from the cache, using the given creation function
to generate a new value.

This function *must* return a value, either from
the cache, or via the given creation function.
If the creation function is called, the newly
created value should be populated into the cache
under the given key before being returned.

```
param key

the value's key.

param creation_function

function that when called generates
a new value.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl.set
documentation:
Place a value in the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param value

the value.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl.get
documentation:
Retrieve a value from the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.
```

methods.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl.invalidate
documentation:
Invalidate a value in the cache.

```
param key

the value's key.

param **kw

cache configuration arguments.
```

class_variables.name: mako.cache.CacheImpl.pass_context
documentation:
If `True`, the {class}`.Context` will be passed to
{meth}`get_or_create <.CacheImpl.get_or_create>` as the name `'context'`.
Provide a cache implementation for use by {class}`.Cache`.

modules.name: mako.lookup
documentation: mako/lookup.py
Copyright 2006-2024 the Mako authors and contributors <see AUTHORS file>
This module is part of Mako and is released under
the MIT License: http://www.opensource.org/licenses/mit-license.php

classes.name: mako.lookup.TemplateCollection

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateCollection.has_template
documentation:
Return `True` if this {class}`.TemplateLookup` is
capable of returning a {class}`.Template` object for the
given `uri`.

```
param uri

String URI of the template to be resolved.
```

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateCollection.get_template
documentation:
Return a {class}`.Template` object corresponding to the given
`uri`.

The default implementation raises
{class}`.NotImplementedError`. Implementations should
raise {class}`.TemplateLookupException` if the given `uri`
cannot be resolved.

```
param uri

String URI of the template to be resolved.

param relativeto

if present, the given uri is assumed to
be relative to this URI.
```

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateCollection.filename_to_uri
documentation:
Convert the given `filename` to a URI relative to
this {class}`.TemplateCollection`.

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateCollection.adjust_uri
documentation:
Adjust the given `uri` based on the calling `filename`.

When this method is called from the runtime, the
`filename` parameter is taken directly to the `filename`
attribute of the calling template. Therefore a custom
{class}`.TemplateCollection` subclass can place any string
identifier desired in the `filename` parameter of the
{class}`.Template` objects it constructs and have them come back
here.

Represent a collection of {class}`.Template` objects,
identifiable via URI.

A {class}`.TemplateCollection` is linked to the usage of
all template tags that address other templates, such
as `<%include>`, `<%namespace>`, and `<%inherit>`.
The `file` attribute of each of those tags refers
to a string URI that is passed to that {class}`.Template`
object's {class}`.TemplateCollection` for resolution.

{class}`.TemplateCollection` is an abstract class,
with the usual default implementation being {class}`.TemplateLookup`.

classes.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup.get_template
documentation:
Return a {class}`.Template` object corresponding to the given
`uri`.

:::{note}
The `relativeto` argument is not supported here at
the moment.
:::

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup.adjust_uri
documentation: Adjust the given `uri` based on the given relative URI.

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup.filename_to_uri
documentation:
Convert the given `filename` to a URI relative to
this {class}`.TemplateCollection`.

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup._relativeize
documentation:
Return the portion of a filename that is 'relative'
to the directories in this lookup.

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup.put_string
documentation:
Place a new {class}`.Template` object into this
{class}`.TemplateLookup`, based on the given string of
`text`.

methods.name: mako.lookup.TemplateLookup.put_template
documentation:
Place a new {class}`.Template` object into this
{class}`.TemplateLookup`, based on the given
{class}`.Template` object.

Represent a collection of templates that locates template source files
from the local filesystem.

The primary argument is the `directories` argument, the list of
directories to search:

```python
lookup = TemplateLookup(["/path/to/templates"])
some_template = lookup.get_template("/index.html")
```

The {class}`.TemplateLookup` can also be given {class}`.Template` objects
programatically using {meth}`.put_string` or {meth}`.put_template`:

```python
lookup = TemplateLookup()
lookup.put_string("base.html", '''
    <html><body>${self.next()}</body></html>
''')
lookup.put_string("hello.html", '''
    <%include file='base.html'/>

    Hello, world !
''')
```

```
param directories

A list of directory names which will be
searched for a particular template URI. The URI is appended
to each directory and the filesystem checked.

param collection_size

Approximate size of the collection used
to store templates. If left at its default of -1, the size
is unbounded, and a plain Python dictionary is used to
relate URI strings to  instances.
Otherwise, a least-recently-used cache object is used which
will maintain the size of the collection approximately to
the number given.

param filesystem_checks

When at its default value of True,
each call to  will
compare the filesystem last modified time to the time in
which an existing  object was created.
This allows the  to regenerate a
new  whenever the original source has
been updated. Set this to False for a very minor
performance increase.

param modulename_callable

A callable which, when present,
is passed the path of the source file as well as the
requested URI, and then returns the full path of the
generated Python module file. This is used to inject
alternate schemes for Python module location. If left at
its default of None, the built in system of generation
based on module_directory plus uri is used.
```

All other keyword parameters available for
{class}`.Template` are mirrored here. When new
{class}`.Template` objects are created, the keywords
established with this {class}`.TemplateLookup` are passed on
to each new {class}`.Template`.

modules.name: mako.template
documentation:
Provides the Template class, a facade for parsing, generating and executing
template strings, as well as template runtime operations.

classes.name: mako.template.Template

methods.name: mako.template.Template.source
documentation: Return the template source code for this {class}`.Template`.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.code
documentation: Return the module source code for this {class}`.Template`.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.render
documentation:
Render the output of this template as a string.

If the template specifies an output encoding, the string
will be encoded accordingly, else the output is raw (raw
output uses `StringIO` and can't handle multibyte
characters). A {class}`.Context` object is created corresponding
to the given data. Arguments that are explicitly declared
by this template's internal rendering method are also
pulled from the given `*args`, `**data` members.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.render_unicode
documentation: Render the output of this template as a unicode object.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.render_context
documentation:
Render this {class}`.Template` with the given context.

The data is written to the context's buffer.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.get_def
documentation: Return a def of this template as a {class}`.DefTemplate`.

methods.name: mako.template.Template.list_defs
documentation:
return a list of defs in the template.

:::{versionadded} 1.0.4
:::

Represents a compiled template.

{class}`.Template` includes a reference to the original
template source (via the {attr}`.source` attribute)
as well as the source code of the
generated Python module (i.e. the {attr}`.code` attribute),
as well as a reference to an actual Python module.

{class}`.Template` is constructed using either a literal string
representing the template text, or a filename representing a filesystem
path to a source file.

```
param text

textual template source.  This argument is mutually
exclusive versus the filename parameter.

param filename

filename of the source template.  This argument is
mutually exclusive versus the text parameter.

param buffer_filters

string list of filters to be applied
to the output of %defs which are buffered, cached, or otherwise
filtered, after all filters
defined with the %def itself have been applied. Allows the
creation of default expression filters that let the output
of return-valued %defs "opt out" of that filtering via
passing special attributes or objects.

param cache_args

Dictionary of cache configuration arguments that
will be passed to the .   See .

param cache_dir

param default_filters

List of string filter names that will
be applied to all expressions.  See .

param enable_loop

When True, enable the loop context variable.
This can be set to False to support templates that may
be making usage of the name "loop".   Individual templates can
re-enable the "loop" context by placing the directive
enable_loop="True" inside the <%page> tag -- see
.

param encoding_errors

Error parameter passed to encode() when
string encoding is performed. See .

param error_handler

Python callable which is called whenever
compile or runtime exceptions occur. The callable is passed
the current context as well as the exception. If the
callable returns True, the exception is considered to
be handled, else it is re-raised after the function
completes. Is used to provide custom error-rendering
functions.

Trying "seealso" as canonical directive name.

source:75: (ERROR/3) Unknown directive type "seealso".

.. seealso::

   :paramref:`.Template.include_error_handler` - include-specific
   error handler function


param format_exceptions

if True, exceptions which occur during
the render phase of this template will be caught and
formatted into an HTML error page, which then becomes the
rendered result of the  call. Otherwise,
runtime exceptions are propagated outwards.

param imports

String list of Python statements, typically individual
"import" lines, which will be placed into the module level
preamble of all generated Python modules. See the example
in .

param future_imports

String list of names to import from .
These will be concatenated into a comma-separated string and inserted
into the beginning of the template, e.g. futures_imports=['FOO',
'BAR'] results in from __future__ import FOO, BAR.  If you're
interested in using features like the new division operator, you must
use future_imports to convey that to the renderer, as otherwise the
import will not appear as the first executed statement in the generated
code and will therefore not have the desired effect.

param include_error_handler

An error handler that runs when this template
is included within another one via the <%include> tag, and raises an
error.  Compare to the  option.

source:106: (INFO/1) No directive entry for "seealso" in module "docutils.parsers.rst.languages.en".
Trying "seealso" as canonical directive name.

source:106: (ERROR/3) Unknown directive type "seealso".

.. seealso::

   :paramref:`.Template.error_handler` - top-level error handler function


param input_encoding

Encoding of the template's source code.  Can
be used in lieu of the coding comment. See
 as well as  for
details on source encoding.

param lookup

a  instance that will be used
for all file lookups via the <%namespace>,
<%include>, and <%inherit> tags. See
.

param module_directory

Filesystem location where generated
Python module files will be placed.

param module_filename

Overrides the filename of the generated
Python module file. For advanced usage only.

param module_writer

A callable which overrides how the Python
module is written entirely.  The callable is passed the
encoded source content of the module and the destination
path to be written to.   The default behavior of module writing
uses a tempfile in conjunction with a file move in order
to make the operation atomic.   So a user-defined module
writing function that mimics the default behavior would be:

import tempfile
import os
import shutil

def module_writer(source, outputpath):
    (dest, name) = \\
        tempfile.mkstemp(
            dir=os.path.dirname(outputpath)
        )

    os.write(dest, source)
    os.close(dest)
    shutil.move(name, outputpath)

from mako.template import Template
mytemplate = Template(
                filename="index.html",
                module_directory="/path/to/modules",
                module_writer=module_writer
            )

The function is provided for unusual configurations where
certain platform-specific permissions or other special
steps are needed.

param output_encoding

The encoding to use when 
is called.
See  as well as .

param preprocessor

Python callable which will be passed
the full template source before it is parsed. The return
result of the callable will be used as the template source
code.

param lexer_cls

A  class used to parse
the template.   The  class is used by
default.

classes.name: mako.template.ModuleTemplate
documentation:
A Template which is constructed given an existing Python module.

e.g.:

```
t = Template("this is a template")
f = file("mymodule.py", "w")
f.write(t.code)
f.close()

import mymodule

t = ModuleTemplate(mymodule)
print(t.render())
```

:copyright: Copyright 2008 by Armin Ronacher.
:license: Python License.

Copyright 2006-2024 the Mako authors and contributors <see AUTHORS file>

functions.name: mako._ast_util.parse
documentation: Parse an expression into an AST node.

functions.name: mako._ast_util.iter_fields
documentation: Iterate over all fields of a node, only yielding existing fields.

classes.name: mako._ast_util.NodeVisitor

methods.name: mako._ast_util.NodeVisitor.get_visitor
documentation:
Return the visitor function for this node or `None` if no visitor
exists for this node.  In that case the generic visit function is
used instead.

methods.name: mako._ast_util.NodeVisitor.visit
documentation: Visit a node.

methods.name: mako._ast_util.NodeVisitor.generic_visit
documentation: Called if no explicit visitor function exists for a node.

Walks the abstract syntax tree and call visitor functions for every node
found.  The visitor functions may return values which will be forwarded
by the `visit` method.

Per default the visitor functions for the nodes are `'visit_'` +
class name of the node.  So a `TryFinally` node visit function would
be `visit_TryFinally`.  This behavior can be changed by overriding
the `get_visitor` function.  If no visitor function exists for a node
(return value `None`) the `generic_visit` visitor is used instead.

Don't use the `NodeVisitor` if you want to apply changes to nodes during
traversing.  For this a special visitor exists (`NodeTransformer`) that
allows modifications.

classes.name: mako._ast_util.NodeTransformer
documentation:
Walks the abstract syntax tree and allows modifications of nodes.

The `NodeTransformer` will walk the AST and use the return value of the
visitor functions to replace or remove the old node.  If the return
value of the visitor function is `None` the node will be removed
from the previous location otherwise it's replaced with the return
value.  The return value may be the original node in which case no
replacement takes place.

Here an example transformer that rewrites all `foo` to `data['foo']`:

```
class RewriteName(NodeTransformer):

    def visit_Name(self, node):
        return copy_location(Subscript(
            value=Name(id='data', ctx=Load()),
            slice=Index(value=Str(s=node.id)),
            ctx=node.ctx
        ), node)
```

Keep in mind that if the node you're operating on has child nodes
you must either transform the child nodes yourself or call the generic
visit function for the node first.

Nodes that were part of a collection of statements (that applies to
all statement nodes) may also return a list of nodes rather than just
a single node.

Usually you use the transformer like this:

```
node = YourTransformer().visit(node)
```

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_toplevel
documentation:
Traverse a template structure for module-level directives and

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_render_callable
documentation:
write a top-level render callable.

this could be the main render() method or that of a top-level def.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_module_code
documentation:
write module-level template code, i.e. that which
is enclosed in <%! %> tags in the template.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_inherit
documentation: write the module-level inheritance-determination callable.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_namespaces
documentation: write the module-level namespace-generating callable.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_variable_declares
documentation:
write variable declarations at the top of a function.

the variable declarations are in the form of callable
definitions for defs and/or name lookup within the
function's context argument. the names declared are based
on the names that are referenced in the function body,
which don't otherwise have any explicit assignment
operation. names that are assigned within the body are
assumed to be locally-scoped variables and are not
separately declared.

for def callable definitions, if the def is a top-level
callable then a 'stub' callable is generated which wraps
the current Context into a closure. if the def is not
top-level, it is fully rendered as a local closure.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_def_decl
documentation: write a locally-available callable referencing a top-level def

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_inline_def
documentation: write a locally-available def callable inside an enclosing def.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_def_finish
documentation:
write the end section of a rendering function, either outermost or
inline.

this takes into account if the rendering function was filtered,
buffered, etc.  and closes the corresponding try: block if any, and
writes code to retrieve captured content, apply filters, send proper
return value.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.write_cache_decorator
documentation:
write a post-function decorator to replace a rendering
callable with a cached version of itself.

methods.name: mako.codegen._GenerateRenderMethod.create_filter_callable
documentation:
write a filter-applying expression based on the filters
present in the given filter names, adjusting for the global
'default' filter aliases as needed.

documentation:
create a Context and return the string
output of the given template and template callable.

functions.name: mako.runtime._exec_template
documentation:
execute a rendering callable given the callable, a
Context, and optional explicit arguments

the contextual Template will be located if it exists, and
the error handling options specified on that Template will
be interpreted here.

classes.name: mako.runtime.Context

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context.lookup
documentation:
Return the {class}`.TemplateLookup` associated
with this {class}`.Context`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context.kwargs
documentation:
Return the dictionary of top level keyword arguments associated
with this {class}`.Context`.

This dictionary only includes the top-level arguments passed to
{meth}`.Template.render`.  It does not include names produced within
the template execution such as local variable names or special names
such as `self`, `next`, etc.

The purpose of this dictionary is primarily for the case that
a {class}`.Template` accepts arguments via its `<%page>` tag,
which are normally expected to be passed via {meth}`.Template.render`,
except the template is being called in an inheritance context,
using the `body()` method.   {attr}`.Context.kwargs` can then be
used to propagate these arguments to the inheriting template:

```
${next.body(**context.kwargs)}
```

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context.push_caller
documentation:
Push a `caller` callable onto the callstack for
this {class}`.Context`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context.pop_caller
documentation:
Pop a `caller` callable onto the callstack for this
{class}`.Context`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context.keys
documentation: Return a list of all names established in this {class}`.Context`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context._push_writer

push a capturing buffer onto this Context and return

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context._pop_buffer_and_writer
documentation:
pop the most recent capturing buffer from this Context
and return the current writer after the pop.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context._push_buffer

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context._pop_buffer
documentation: pop the most recent capturing buffer from this Context.

Write a string to this {class}`.Context` object's

Create a new {class}`.Context` with a copy of this

The {attr}`.Context.kwargs` collection remains

methods.name: mako.runtime.Context._clean_inheritance_tokens
documentation:
create a new copy of this {class}`.Context`. with
tokens related to inheritance state removed.

Provides runtime namespace, output buffer, and various
callstacks for templates.

See {ref}`runtime_toplevel` for detail on the usage of
{class}`.Context`.

classes.name: mako.runtime.Undefined
documentation:
Represents an undefined value in a template.

All template modules have a constant value
`UNDEFINED` present which is an instance of this
object.

classes.name: mako.runtime.LoopStack
documentation:
a stack for LoopContexts that implements the context manager protocol
to automatically pop off the top of the stack on context exit

classes.name: mako.runtime.LoopContext

methods.name: mako.runtime.LoopContext.cycle
documentation: Cycle through values as the loop progresses.

A magic loop variable.
Automatically accessible in any `% for` block.

See the section {ref}`loop_context` for usage
notes.

{attr}`parent` -> {class}`.LoopContext` or `None`

: The parent loop, if one exists.

{attr}`index` -> `int`

: The 0-based iteration count.

{attr}`reverse_index` -> `int`

: The number of iterations remaining.

{attr}`first` -> `bool`

: `True` on the first iteration, `False` otherwise.

{attr}`last` -> `bool`

: `True` on the last iteration, `False` otherwise.

{attr}`even` -> `bool`

: `True` when `index` is even.

{attr}`odd` -> `bool`

: `True` when `index` is odd.

classes.name: mako.runtime.Namespace

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.attr
documentation:
Access module level attributes by name.

This accessor allows templates to supply "scalar"
attributes which are particularly handy in inheritance
relationships.

:::{seealso}
{ref}`inheritance_attr`

{ref}`namespace_attr_for_includes`
:::

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.get_namespace
documentation:
Return a {class}`.Namespace` corresponding to the given `uri`.

If the given `uri` is a relative URI (i.e. it does not
contain a leading slash `/`), the `uri` is adjusted to
be relative to the `uri` of the namespace itself. This
method is therefore mostly useful off of the built-in
`local` namespace, described in {ref}`namespace_local`.

In
most cases, a template wouldn't need this function, and
should instead use the `<%namespace>` tag to load
namespaces. However, since all `<%namespace>` tags are
evaluated before the body of a template ever runs,
this method can be used to locate namespaces using
expressions that were generated within the body code of
the template, or to conditionally use a particular
namespace.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.get_template
documentation:
Return a {class}`.Template` from the given `uri`.

The `uri` resolution is relative to the `uri` of this
{class}`.Namespace` object's {class}`.Template`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.get_cached
documentation:
Return a value from the {class}`.Cache` referenced by this
{class}`.Namespace` object's {class}`.Template`.

The advantage to this method versus direct access to the
{class}`.Cache` is that the configuration parameters
declared in `<%page>` take effect here, thereby calling
up the same configured backend as that configured
by `<%page>`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.cache
documentation:
Return the {class}`.Cache` object referenced
by this {class}`.Namespace` object's
{class}`.Template`.

methods.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.include_file
documentation: Include a file at the given `uri`.

class_variables.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.module
documentation:
The Python module referenced by this {class}`.Namespace`.

If the namespace references a {class}`.Template`, then
this module is the equivalent of `template.module`,
i.e. the generated module for the template.

class_variables.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.template
documentation:
The {class}`.Template` object referenced by this
{class}`.Namespace`, if any.

class_variables.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.context
documentation:
The {class}`.Context` object for this {class}`.Namespace`.

Namespaces are often created with copies of contexts that
contain slightly different data, particularly in inheritance
scenarios. Using the {class}`.Context` off of a {class}`.Namespace` one
can traverse an entire chain of templates that inherit from
one-another.

class_variables.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.filename
documentation:
The path of the filesystem file used for this
{class}`.Namespace`'s module or template.

If this is a pure module-based
{class}`.Namespace`, this evaluates to `module.__file__`. If a
template-based namespace, it evaluates to the original
template file location.

class_variables.name: mako.runtime.Namespace.uri
documentation:
The URI for this {class}`.Namespace`'s template.

I.e. whatever was sent to {meth}`.TemplateLookup.get_template()`.

This is the equivalent of {attr}`.Template.uri`.

Provides access to collections of rendering methods, which
can be local, from other templates, or from imported modules.

To access a particular rendering method referenced by a
{class}`.Namespace`, use plain attribute access:

```mako
${some_namespace.foo(x, y, z)}
```

{class}`.Namespace` also contains several built-in attributes
described here.

classes.name: mako.runtime.TemplateNamespace

methods.name: mako.runtime.TemplateNamespace.module
documentation:
The Python module referenced by this {class}`.Namespace`.

If the namespace references a {class}`.Template`, then
this module is the equivalent of `template.module`,
i.e. the generated module for the template.

methods.name: mako.runtime.TemplateNamespace.filename
documentation:
The path of the filesystem file used for this
{class}`.Namespace`'s module or template.

methods.name: mako.runtime.TemplateNamespace.uri
documentation:
The URI for this {class}`.Namespace`'s template.

I.e. whatever was sent to {meth}`.TemplateLookup.get_template()`.

This is the equivalent of {attr}`.Template.uri`.
A {class}`.Namespace` specific to a {class}`.Template` instance.

classes.name: mako.runtime.ModuleNamespace

methods.name: mako.runtime.ModuleNamespace.filename
documentation:
The path of the filesystem file used for this
{class}`.Namespace`'s module or template.
A {class}`.Namespace` specific to a Python module instance.
